stories.headline: Employee of Aurora Immigrant Detention Center Arrested After Shooting Protester Outside Facility
stories.summary:
An employee of an immigrant detention center in Aurora was arrested after allegedly shooting a protester outside the facility Thursday night, according to Denver7. The shooting occurred during what appeared to be a demonstration at the detention facility.

Details on the protester's condition and the specific charges filed against the employee were not immediately available. The incident adds to ongoing tensions surrounding immigration enforcement facilities in the Denver metro area.

Aurora and the surrounding region have seen repeated protests at immigration detention sites in recent months. Authorities are continuing to investigate the circumstances of the shooting.

stories.headline: Man Dies in Denver Police Custody Following Attempted Carjacking Near Cheesman Park
stories.summary:
A man suspected of an attempted carjacking near Cheesman Park died after being arrested by Denver police Tuesday afternoon, the department announced Thursday. Officers responded to 911 calls about a fight in the 2100 block of East Colfax Avenue at 4:22 p.m., including a report that a man in his underwear had entered a vehicle and attempted to take it.

After being pulled from the vehicle by occupants who struck him on the head and upper body, the man entered a nearby business and caused a disturbance. Officers handcuffed and subdued him as he struggled; officials said no Tasers or pepper spray were used and the man had no visible injuries. Emergency medical crews transported him to a hospital, where he was later pronounced dead.

The Colorado State Patrol, Colorado Bureau of Investigation and Denver Police Department's major crimes division are jointly investigating the case, with oversight from the city's independent monitor's office. The Denver medical examiner's office will release the man's identity and cause of death.

stories.headline: Update: Arapahoe County Fire District to Charge $5,000 Recovery Fee for Future Missile Silo Rescues
stories.summary:
Following two rescues in less than a week at an abandoned missile silo in Arapahoe County, the Sable Altura Fire District has announced it will begin charging a $5,000 recovery fee for future rescues at the site. The fee is not a fine but is designed to help cover the costs of specialized equipment and training required for the technically demanding extractions.

The first rescue on July 8 involved two juveniles and an 18-year-old woman who entered the underground structure via a rope ladder but could not climb out. A second rescue on July 14 involved a 19-year-old man and a 28-year-old woman, with the woman recovered from approximately 60 feet down the shaft. All individuals involved face potential second-degree criminal trespassing charges, which carry penalties of up to 10 days in jail, a fine of up to $300, or both.

Law enforcement officials are urging the public to stop attempting to enter abandoned missile silos, citing inherent dangers to both trespassers and rescue personnel. The $5,000 recovery fee will apply to anyone requiring extraction after illegally entering the structure going forward.

stories.headline: Denver City Councilwoman Shontel Lewis Files to Run for Mayor in 2027
stories.summary:
Denver City Councilwoman Shontel Lewis officially filed paperwork Thursday to run for mayor in 2027, announcing her candidacy in a video posted Friday on Instagram. Lewis, who represents District 8 covering Central Park, Park Hill and Montbello, will challenge incumbent Mayor Mike Johnston, who has indicated he intends to seek a second term.

Lewis was endorsed by the local chapter of the Democratic Socialists of America in her 2023 council race and is expected to run as a progressive challenger. Her entry into the race follows the stunning primary victory of DSA-backed Melat Kiros over longtime U.S. Rep. Diana DeGette on June 30, a result that energized the local progressive movement. Repeat progressive candidate Lisa Calderón is also among the declared challengers to Johnston.

Because she cannot concurrently run for reelection to her council seat, Lewis's mayoral filing means she will not seek another term representing District 8. Before joining the council, she served as an elected RTD director and as vice president at the Colorado Coalition for the Homeless. Johnston's spokesman issued a response to her announcement Friday, though the mayor has not yet formally filed for reelection.

stories.headline: Mayor Johnston to Focus State of the City Address on Affordability and Child Care
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston will deliver his third State of the City address Tuesday, centering his remarks on affordability and child care as the defining priorities for the final year of his current term. Johnston told the Denver Gazette in an exclusive interview that housing costs remain the city's most significant burden for families, while also pointing to utilities and child care as major financial pressures.

Johnston said the city is working on a future agreement with Xcel Energy to help manage utility costs long-term and will outline plans to build a more accessible and affordable child care system, with a particular emphasis on infants and toddlers. The Denver Preschool Program, which serves 3- and 4-year-olds, was cited as a model the city hopes to expand upon, though observers have noted that published city goals do not yet include specific funding commitments.

On homelessness, Johnston said he has no regrets about the nearly $200 million Denver has spent on housing immigrants, clearing encampments and establishing shelters, calling the result the largest reduction in street homelessness in American history. The State of the City address comes as Johnston prepares to face a 2027 reelection challenge, with Councilwoman Shontel Lewis filing her mayoral campaign the same day as the Gazette's interview was published.

stories.headline: Boebert's Democratic Challenger Holds 10-to-1 Fundraising Advantage in Colorado's 4th District Race
stories.summary:
Retired U.S. Navy Rear Admiral Eileen Laubacher, the Democratic candidate in Colorado's 4th Congressional District, ended June with $3.6 million cash on hand compared to just $247,000 for incumbent Republican Rep. Lauren Boebert, according to new Federal Election Commission filings. Over the full election cycle, Laubacher has raised nearly $10.3 million to Boebert's $1.1 million.

Political analysts caution that the money gap may not translate into a competitive race given the district's deep-red partisan lean. The nonpartisan Cook Political Report gives Republicans a roughly 9-percentage-point advantage in the district, which covers Colorado's Eastern Plains and Douglas County. Republicans also outnumber Democrats by more than 100,000 registered voters.

Colorado State University political science professor Kyle Saunders said history shows money alone rarely overcomes a structural partisan advantage of that magnitude without a national wave, major scandal, or candidate collapse. Boebert's fundraising is notably weaker than her previous campaigns — she had approximately $530,000 cash on hand at the same point in 2024 and nearly $2.3 million in mid-2022. The November general election will determine whether Laubacher's financial edge can overcome the district's Republican tilt.

stories.headline: Colorado Wildfires: Aspen Acres Fire Crosses 100,000 Acres as Drought Complicates Firefighting Landscape
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres fire in southern Colorado has grown to more than 99,820 acres and is 48 percent contained, with more than 775 structures destroyed in Custer and Pueblo counties. More than 1,700 personnel were working the fire as of Thursday, with mandatory evacuations still in place for communities near the Pueblo-Custer county border, including Rye and San Isabel. Roughly 3,800 homes and 11,000 residents have been affected.

Three other major fires continue to burn across the state. The Gold Mountain fire northeast of Ouray has burned 37,734 acres and is 13 percent contained, and claimed the life of Canadian helicopter pilot Nicholas Dale, 56, when his aircraft crashed into Silver Jack Reservoir on July 12. The Ferris fire in southwestern Colorado has burned 64,869 acres and is 53 percent contained, while the human-caused Willow fire near Leadville has burned 6,877 acres and is 36 percent contained.

Despite severe drought conditions that have reduced statewide reservoir storage to about 65 percent of normal, fire officials say access to water has not limited firefighting operations. A March heat wave ended Colorado's snowpack accumulation season a month early, leaving landscapes as dry as they would typically be in mid-August. About 90 percent of the state remains under drought conditions, according to the U.S. Drought Monitor.

stories.headline: Colorado Parks and Wildlife Commission Imposes Two-Per-Day Bag Limit on Furbearer Trapping
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife commissioners voted 6-5 Thursday to limit hunters and trappers to killing two individuals per day from each of the state's 17 huntable furbearer species, including beavers, foxes, bobcats and mink. The new daily bag limit is stricter than the options presented by CPW staff, who told commissioners there was no biological evidence that current trapping levels were harming any species' populations.

Commissioners acknowledged the rule is unlikely to change trapper behavior in practice, as most take only a few animals per year, but said the limit was needed to address what some view as a regulatory loophole allowing unlimited daily kills. Commissioner Becky Niemiec framed the debate as one of values rather than biology. Critics from the hunting community argued the change was unnecessary and called for the commission to adopt one of the less restrictive options proposed by CPW staff.

The furbearer bag limit decision is part of a broader ongoing debate over hunting regulations in Colorado. Wildlife advocacy groups have pursued several recent restrictions, including a failed ballot initiative to ban mountain lion and bobcat hunting. In response, some hunting groups are pursuing a November ballot measure to enshrine hunting and fishing rights in the state constitution.

stories.headline: Denver Area Starter Home Market Remains Elusive as Entry-Level Prices Hold Near $400,000
stories.summary:
Despite hopes for price corrections, the least expensive single-family homes in Colorado now start at around $400,000, leaving a generation of would-be first-time buyers struggling to enter the market. The Denver Gazette followed one couple, who relocated from Washington, D.C., as they explored options in neighborhoods from Virginia Village to Wheat Ridge, finding that even remodeled mid-century ranch homes in historically affordable tracts are listing well above $600,000.

Real estate agents report that buyers are being forced to weigh the long-term value of homeownership against continuing to rent and investing a down payment in financial markets instead. The couple featured in the story declined an asking price of $639,000 — reduced from an original $670,000 — after an initial offer was rejected, illustrating the difficult calculus facing buyers in a market with limited inventory at accessible price points.

Analysts had expected rising interest rates and broader economic pressures to cool Denver-area home prices more significantly. Instead, constrained supply has kept prices elevated, particularly at the lower end of the market. The situation mirrors national affordability challenges but is acute along the Front Range, where strong demand and limited land have historically pushed prices higher than in comparable metros.

stories.headline: Denver Summit FC Opens Centennial Stadium Saturday Against Portland Thorns in Marquee NWSL Matchup
stories.summary:
Denver Summit FC will open its new 12,000-seat Centennial Stadium on Saturday against the Portland Thorns in what team officials are calling a marquee event for the young NWSL franchise. The match takes on added significance with the official rostering of U.S. Women's National Team captain and World Cup champion Lindsey Heaps, a Golden native who became eligible for play after the NWSL international transfer window opened Tuesday.

The game doubles as a reunion of Colorado-raised talent. Heaps and Portland forward Sophia Wilson, from Windsor, were teammates on both the Portland Thorns and the USWNT, including at the 2023 Women's World Cup and the gold-medal-winning 2024 Olympic squad. Denver defender Janine Sonis, a Highlands Ranch product, is also on the field for the home side. Wilson returned to the Thorns roster in March after the birth of her daughter.

Summit FC president Jen Millet called it a dream matchup for the franchise's stadium opener. Portland enters as the stronger side on paper, sitting at 8-3-4 with 27 points compared to Denver's 4-4-5 record and 16 points. Heaps acknowledged Wilson is a focal point for the Thorns' attack and said her team will need to work hard to contain her while also hoping to recruit the forward to Denver after Wilson's contract with Portland expires.

stories.headline: Avalanche Hire Finnish Coach Jussi Ahokas to Lead AHL Affiliate Colorado Eagles
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche hired Jussi Ahokas, 45, as the new head coach of their AHL affiliate Colorado Eagles on Friday, bringing in one of the most decorated young coaches in North American hockey. Ahokas led the Kitchener Rangers to the OHL title and the Memorial Cup championship this past season after winning OHL Coach of the Year honors in 2024-25.

A native of Oulu, Finland, Ahokas previously spent four seasons coaching in Finland's SM-Liiga before joining Kitchener. He also led Finland to gold medals at the 2019 IIHF World Junior Championship and the 2016 U-18 World Championship, and has worked with NHL players including Juraj Slafkovsky, Miro Heiskanen and Kaapo Kakko at the club or international level. Avalanche prospect Christian Humphreys played under Ahokas at Kitchener for the past two seasons.

Ahokas becomes the fourth head coach of the Eagles since the franchise joined the AHL as Colorado's affiliate. His three predecessors — Greg Cronin, Aaron Schneekloth and Mark Letestu — all moved on to NHL positions, with Letestu departing last month for an assistant coaching role with the Vegas Golden Knights after taking the Eagles to the Western Conference Final.

stories.headline: Rockies Open Second-Half Homestand Against Reds as Colorado Looks to Regroup
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies opened an important home series against the Cincinnati Reds at Coors Field on Friday, kicking off what the Denver Gazette described as a significant homestand as the team enters the unofficial second half of the season. Three games against Cincinnati are scheduled before the Rockies host another three-game series.

The homestand comes at a pivotal juncture for a Rockies franchise that has struggled to establish consistency in recent seasons. Home games at Coors Field carry particular weight given the ballpark's historically offense-friendly conditions.

Details on the results of Friday's opener were not available at press time. The series gives Colorado an opportunity to build momentum heading into the summer stretch of the schedule.

weather.notable: Nearly 5 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I like a woman with a head on her shoulders. Because I hate necks.
